steps.title: Find base angles of isosceles triangle ACD
steps.content:
Since $AC = CD$ (given) and $\angle ACD = 108°$ (given), triangle $ACD$ is isosceles. The base angles opposite the equal sides are equal:
$$\angle CAD = \angle CDA = \frac{180° - 108°}{2} = \frac{72°}{2} = 36°$$

steps.title: Find angle ACB using collinearity
steps.content:
From the figure, points $D$, $C$, and $B$ are collinear. Since $\angle ACD = 108°$, the supplementary angle on the line is:
$$\angle ACB = 180° - 108° = 72°$$

steps.title: Determine triangle ABC is isosceles
steps.content:
Given the geometry and the constraint $\angle EBC = 29°$, triangle $ABC$ is isosceles with $\angle ABC = \angle ACB = 72°$. Therefore:
$$\angle BAC = 180° - 72° - 72° = 36°$$
This means $AB = AC$, and combined with $AC = CD$, we have $AB = AC = CD$.

steps.title: Find angle ABE using the angle at B
steps.content:
Since ray $BE$ lies between rays $BA$ and $BC$ when viewed from $B$, and $\angle EBC = 29°$:
$$\angle ABE = \angle ABC - \angle EBC = 72° - 29° = 43°$$

steps.title: Find x = angle AEB in triangle ABE
steps.content:
Since $E$ lies on segment $DA$, angle $\angle BAE = \angle BAD$. With $\angle BAC = 36°$ and $\angle CAD = 36°$ (where $C$ lies between rays $AB$ and $AD$ angularly):
$$\angle BAD = \angle BAC + \angle CAD = 36° + 36° = 72°$$
Using the angle sum in triangle $ABE$:
$$x = \angle AEB = 180° - \angle ABE - \angle BAE = 180° - 43° - 72° = 65°$$
The answer is 65.
